old_exact: [¶24] When reviewing the denial of a motion to suppress, this Court: "defers to the district court's findings of fact and resolves conflicts in testimony in favor of affirmance. Generally, a district court's decision to deny a motion to suppress will be affirmed if there is the sufficient competent evidence capable of supporting court's findings and if its decision is not contrary to the manifest weight of the evidence. Questions of law are fully reviewable on appeal, and whether a finding of fact meets a legal standard is a question of law." State v. Canfield, 2013 ND 236, ¶ 6, 840 N.W.2d 620 (cleaned up).
new_exact: [¶24] When reviewing the denial of a motion to suppress, this Court: "defers to the district court's findings of fact and resolves conflicts in testimony in favor of affirmance. Generally, a district court's decision to deny a motion to suppress will be affirmed if there is sufficient competent evidence capable of supporting the court's findings and if its decision is not contrary to the manifest weight of the evidence. Questions of law are fully reviewable on appeal, and whether a finding of fact meets a legal standard is a question of law." State v. Canfield, 2013 ND 236, ¶ 6, 840 N.W.2d 620 (cleaned up).
source: 2025ND65.pdf, lines 403-408 (block quote of State v. Canfield precedent)
note: Two ingestion errors in the quoted text from State v. Canfield: (1) will be affirmed if there is the sufficient should omit the before sufficient; (2) supporting court's findings should include the before court's. Both confirmed in authoritative PDF.
